---
title: WRDS to Parquet
---

Use this path when the source data live in WRDS PostgreSQL and the destination
is a local Parquet file repository.

## Main helpers

- `wrds_update_pq(table_name, schema, ...)` only rewrites a Parquet file when
  the WRDS source is newer.
- `wrds_sql_to_pq(sql, table_name, schema, ...)` exports a custom WRDS SQL query into the standard Parquet layout.
- `wrds_update_schema(schema, ...)` updates a full WRDS schema into Parquet.
- `wrds_pg_to_pq(table_name, schema, ...)` exports one WRDS table to Parquet, but without the update logic of `wrds_update_pq()`.

## Typical first run

```python
from db2pq import wrds_update_pq

wrds_update_pq("dsi", "crsp")
```

If `WRDS_ID` is not configured yet, `db2pq` can prompt for it and suggest
adding it to a local `.env` file. If the WRDS PostgreSQL password is not yet in
`.pgpass`, `db2pq` can prompt for it and save it for later use.

## More examples

A CRSP-style export where identifier columns are normalized to integer types:

```python
from db2pq import wrds_update_pq

wrds_update_pq(
    "ccmxpf_lnkhist",
    "crsp",
    col_types={"lpermno": "int32", "lpermco": "int32"},
)
```

## Using SAS metadata with `use_sas=True`

Use this path when the WRDS PostgreSQL table comment is missing or not the
freshness signal you want. In this mode, `db2pq` checks SAS-side metadata
instead.

A RavenPack-style example where the WRDS PostgreSQL schema and the SAS metadata
schema do not line up:

```python
from db2pq import wrds_update_pq

wrds_update_pq(
    "rpa_entity_mappings",
    "ravenpack_common",
    use_sas=True,
    sas_schema="rpa",
)
```

You can also combine `use_sas=True` with explicit timestamp coercions when
exporting annual RavenPack tables:

```python
wrds_update_pq(
    "rpa_djpr_equities_2024",
    "ravenpack_dj",
    use_sas=True,
    sas_schema="rpa",
    batched=False,
    col_types={
        "timestamp_utc": "timestamp",
        "rpa_time_utc": "string",
        "event_start_date_utc": "timestamp",
        "event_end_date_utc": "timestamp",
    },
)
```

## Common options

- `engine="duckdb"` uses DuckDB to read from PostgreSQL.
- `engine="adbc"` streams Arrow batches directly from PostgreSQL.
- `where=` applies a SQL filter before export.
- `keep=` and `drop=` filter source columns with regex patterns.
- `obs=` limits rows for testing.
- `archive=True` moves replaced Parquet files into an archive directory.
- `use_sas=True` can use SAS metadata for update detection when PostgreSQL
  comments are missing or incomplete.

## Output layout

Files are written as:

```text
<DATA_DIR>/<schema>/<table>.parquet
```

## When To Use The Web Query

The WRDS web query can be useful when you are trying to identify the exact
table behind a dataset or want WRDS to draft a starting SQL query for you.
That is often a practical way to move from the WRDS website to a
`wrds_sql_to_pq(...)` workflow.

## Related reference pages

- [wrds_pg_to_pq](reference/wrds_pg_to_pq.qmd)
- [wrds_sql_to_pq](reference/wrds_sql_to_pq.qmd)
- [wrds_update_pq](reference/wrds_update_pq.qmd)
- [wrds_update_schema](reference/wrds_update_schema.qmd)
- [pq_last_modified](reference/pq_last_modified.qmd)

## Related guides

- [Using the WRDS web query](web-query.qmd)
